item.title: so here it is
item.content:encoded: I just knew I would find a toilet paper scp around here somewhere, and there you go, and an awesome one just as well! Love the physics you managed to slot into this, never thought a toilet paper could be so fucking menacing +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This skip is good just because it avoids the hurdle of oh so many "stupid and impossible science" skips: Making it a Prometheus Labs thing. For that I thank you. Upvoted

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What gets me about this skip, the absolute best part, I think, is that this mad scientist apparently discovered the secret to losslessly converting energy into matter and back, and the first and only thing they think to do with this world-shaking discovery is…make infinite toilet paper. On top of that, what really sealed the deal for me was the fact that I discovered it in the midst of the 2020 Coronavirus Pandemic/Toilet Paper Shortage. +1ed with extreme prejudice.

item.title: Re: I don't know
item.content:encoded: I assume they can't automate the process as once the machine has it's energy sapped it's an immediate danger to the facility, which would require a higher level of effort to deal with than just a cyclical procedure.

item.title: I don't know
item.content:encoded:
I don't understand this section:
"This has happened twice during power failures. Both times, onsite staff have sacrificed themselves to remove ten sheets, killing themselves through hypothermia instantly. While not a sustainable containment solution, such a sacrifice has prevented SCP-2966 from going critical."

To much was removed, caused energy to be syphoned, area hit near absolute zero, and somehow staff removing more sheets resets it? Is this because to many sheets can weigh it down and un spool it's self? If not being used heats itself up why not just wait till it auto stabilizes and start the procedure again? Better yet, don't use D-class, use automation, make a belt feed pull it slowly out of the roll at approx 1.25 sheets per hour. And Don't worry about using it. Feed it straight into a furnace to power the whole thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just remove the logs but leave how it was recovered, I like the image it makes

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The main description was highly engaging, it reminded me strongly in a good way of the cold fusion paper towel dispenser. However, none of the collapsibles added anything to the article and I feel like it would be stronger and more concise without them. This is weird enough to stand on its own.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Last but one paragraph.

If not used, it risks matter to energy conversion, releasing all the energy it has contained.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a good SCP, but why is it Keter? As long as it isn't used by anyone and no sheets are generated, it isn't doing anything, right? So as long as no one uses it, it would be Safe, no? Unless it constantly uses up energy to generate toilet paper. If so, well… that's amazing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is the exact sort of bizarre that brought me to this site in the first place. Keep it up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I largely agree with this. The inspiration behind creating an infinite roll of toilet paper is entirely obvious. I mean, this is classic cursed-wish-granter territory on a really trivial level. The challenge of this kind if article is making the reader take the consequences seriously in spite of the dumb premise.

I was already leaning it against taking it seriously in spite of the really deep physics, and the logs just obliterated that.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So many great ideas begin with "what if?"

When you put "a roll of toilet paper could destroy the world" after it, something wonderful happened. I was waiting for the reveal in which we learn *why* it can't just be put in a box , and was not disappointed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 2 megatons.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm too lazy to calculate it precisely, but I'm pretty sure your math is off. 1e16j is on the order of megatons.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, I can dig the physics that are in play in this article, but the log just comes across as very ham handed and just break my suspension of disbelief as a reader.

-1.

I hope my downvote doesn't leave you too down in the dumps.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Noting that I have removed the so-called "object" tag from the tags used here, since "object" is not a recognised tag under the site's Tag Guide. While authors can choose to tag their own articles, I will recommend to adhere to the Tag Guide strictly and make sure all the needed tags are there. If not, there are staff who will tag completely untagged articles on your behalf. But incorrectly tagged articles will be off their radar, since a bot is used to detect such articles.

If one feels that a tag never used before is necessary, please consult a relevant member of the staff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the physics here, but little else, and downvoted at the hammy "nether regions" line.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What gets me is that rather than making infinite gold or infinite oil or something valuable, the makes and infinite roll of toilet paper.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's infinite toilet paper.

It's Keter.

My night is made.

I adore the technobabble in the explanation log, don't change a thing. XD

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't care for articles in which some random person somehow learns on their own to violate the rules of physics, and then uses their newfound godlike ability to make something completely goofy. Other examples of this would be the cold fusion paper towel dispenser and the whiteboards full of dog organs that teach you German. This is the fallacy in the "add backstory into it" reaction that a lot of people have to everything; in articles where the object itself is driving the narrative, the explanation is almost always less satisfying than the mystery as contemplated by the reader. Especially if that explanation comes down to "goofball with a get-rich-quick scheme." However, I do get that there are fans of this sort of thing, so this is more of a personal element to my vote rather than a pronouncement of deficient writing.

That said, I do think that the formulaic approach to this is a negative factor. We constantly tell everyone to read the other articles on the site, which is advice that I agree with, but the downside to that is that it reinforces certain elements that appear in a ton of other articles to the point where it just gets silly to see them over and over again. Here, you've got the standard recovery narrative, conveniently extant journal detailing this guy's thought process, and an incident because of course someone is going to blow themselves up. The predictable, move-down-the-checklist feeling I got from this, right down to the officially denied request to use the skip, made this feel less like a standout article worthy of attention and more like a rote execution of something I've seen a hundred times previous.

Even though I don't care for the core idea, it seems like there has to be better ways to tell the story. Does it have be a journal left lying around? Can't they have dug up some video of a sales pitch to investors or some tax filings or some other documentation that would give the reader the information you want them to have? I'm not sure I could come to an upvote on this, but it really could have benefited from pushing against the old established practices a bit.
Due to the lack of mad control groups, mad peer review, and a decent mad theoretical base, mad science is, essentially, mad engineering.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The special containment procedures (emphasis on the 'special') were very well thought out and very immersive. I love when an article doesn't cop out in the scientific tone and precise specification in this section. I'm going through, getting more and more intrigued with what horrible, physics-defying anomaly could possibly require these procedures…and then I get to the first line of the description, and immediately upvoted.

The logs themselves are fine, I believe, though you may be able to expand the Foundation's notes of the Doctor Blackbox, to further lampshade how absolutely nonsensical his technoabble is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What you said makes me think that the scientist who created this skip is incredibly shortsighted and just plain greedy. Also, those technobabble names sound kind of believable when it's coming from this chump's mouth. They could have been called something else.

Edit: On second thought, I think making the guy sound too much like a fool is a detriment to the article. Also, this could do without the log anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Based on the above comments, I'm likely the least intelligent poster here. That said, I really liked this SCP. Dr. ██████████ was a man who understood the struggle of a world with finite toilet paper, and he died pursuing a noble goal. For that, I salute him.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really, really want to +1 this. I love the idea, especially considering just how often we see SCPs generating matter from nowhere willy-nilly. I really like seeing one that actually contemplates what that kind of matter generation would require under currently-understood physical law.

However, I have to agree with A Random Day and MrWrong. The log is just far too convenient, a nice sheaf of exposition just lying around. Furthermore, it's quite a bit too goofy for my tastes, especially all that business about "halved hafnium." It seems like it takes a guy who has only a very slim understanding of basic science at all, and then just drowns his log in science words. That said, I actually do really like the idea of him misusing E=mc^2 and massively underestimating the energy required.

No vote for now, but if you changed the circumstances and tone of those logs a bit, I feel that I'd be more inclined to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good that it has been cleaned up. However, I'm not sure if your new series of notes are a true improvement. The talk on "quarternium" and "halved hafnium" feels like techno-babble, especially since the latter is a metal and the SCP is in itself paper. Like, how can hafnium be halved? In what sense is it halved? Yes, even though SCPs defy logic by convention, I think I've reached my limit for this article.

And given that "quarternium" does not exist in Nature, how would the Foundation know it has to be an element?

Overall, the note puts a rather tried story of someone over-estimating themselves in the scientific field.

And I got to question the creator's logic that an infinite toilet paper roll could be his ticket to wealth. If such a thing exists and distributed en masse, the demand to buy toilet papers is mostly gone since the supply is endless. Of course, there is always the question of affordability for these revolutionary devices. But would one use such a thing, as compared to mundane toilet paper?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The thread will really be flush with it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There is going to be so much toilet humor in this thread I just know it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Neutral vote. Toilet paper that literally converts energy to mass is good. Convenient explanatory journal done in a way that isn't as organic as SCP-2102 or SCP-1984 is bad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1 as dictated by my "anything that makes me sit there for more than 60 seconds trying to process the full ramifications of gets an upvote" rule.

Addendum: On further processing, I think what I like so much here is the fact that Dr Blackbox is so deep into Time Cube territory that he's taken a chunk of reality with him. The logs are really what make this for me- the combination of vaguely-not-entirely-wrong technobabble and inductive reasoning paint a great picture of a guy who's gone totally off the deep end into madness, but remains blissfully unaware. The difference between this guy and any other otherwise competent scientist suffering a complete mental breakdown and writing a grand unified theory based on E=𝛑squirrels? This idea worked.

On a side note, for those having trouble buying into the logs, I present a thorough explanation of why gases aren't affected by gravity and this proves Earth is the center of the universe and stuff, and that the entire scientific community is a bunch of morons for not realizing these obvious facts. This guy has taken out ads in numerous high-profile science magazines over the past couple years.
